questionText: Prove that the sum of any four consecutive integers is even.

steps.description: Let the first integer be general.
steps.explanation: Using $n$ for the first integer keeps the argument valid for every starting value.
Consecutive integers rise by $1$, so the four numbers are $n$ through $n+3$.
The claim is about their total, so we sum the four integers.
Four $n$ terms give $4n$, and the constants $1+2+3$ give $6$.
Both terms are even, so factoring $2$ shows the sum is twice an integer.

steps.description: Identify the bracket as an integer.
steps.explanation: Since $n$ is an integer, $2n+3$ is an integer, so $S=2\times(\text{integer})$.
The sum equals $2(2n+3)$, which is even by definition, so the sum of any four consecutive integers is even.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $n+(n+1)+(n+2)+(n+3)=4n+6=2(2n+3)$, which is even. Hence the sum of any four consecutive integers is even. $\blacksquare$
